common_name: Trumpet Creeper (Trumpet Vine, Cow Itch Vine)

planting.note: Plant container stock or rooted cuttings in spring in well-drained to dry, poor to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; trumpet creeper is an exceptionally vigorous native vine that can overwhelm weaker support structures and spread aggressively — give it a sturdy masonry wall, fence, or large arbor and do not plant near small trees or delicate plants.

directions: Plant in poor to moderately fertile, well-drained to dry soil in full sun on a very sturdy support; never plant on wooden fences or near small trees as the vigorous vine can destroy them within a few years. Campsis radicans (trumpet creeper) is a native deciduous woody vine of exceptional ornamental value and hummingbird attraction — its large, trumpeted, 3-inch orange-red to scarlet flowers bloom prolifically in clusters of 4–12 from July through September on the current season's growth, creating an irresistible magnet for ruby-throated hummingbirds which are the primary pollinators. The vine climbs by clinging rootlets that attach firmly to masonry, bark, and wood and can scale heights of 20–40 feet or more. It is one of the most vigorous plants in American horticulture — spreading by root suckers and self-seeding freely — and should be sited with awareness of its potential to overtake its space: do not plant on wooden structures, near small trees, or where spread cannot be controlled. The cultivar 'Flamenco' has yellow-orange flowers; 'Crimson Trumpet' has deeper red flowers; 'Indian Summer' is more compact. Despite its vigor, trumpet creeper is extraordinarily valuable for wildlife and native garden plantings on large, sturdy supports.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Native Vines

planting.note: Plant container stock or rooted cuttings in spring in well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun to part shade; purple passionflower spreads by root suckers and is very drought-tolerant once established — do not plant in rich, wet soil, which promotes rank vegetative growth over flowering and fruiting.

directions: Plant in lean, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun on a fence, trellis, or wire support; allow some room for root sucker spread. Passiflora incarnata (purple passionflower or maypop) is the cold-hardy native passionflower of the eastern United States, producing the most extraordinarily ornate flowers in temperate horticulture — each 3-inch flower displays five white petals and five sepals framed by a crown of lavender to purple, fringed, radiating filaments arranged in concentric rings, the whole structure appearing more like an alien artifact than a flower. Flowers bloom from June through September and are followed by edible, golf-ball-sized, yellow-green to orange fruits (maypops) with a sweet-tart, guava-like flavor used for jams, jellies, and drinks. The vine is the obligate host plant for Gulf Fritillary and Zebra Longwing butterfly larvae — caterpillars consume the leaves, making it exceptional for butterfly gardens. Passiflora incarnata is cold-hardy to zone 5 (dying to the ground in winter but resprouting vigorously from roots), and in zones 7–10 it is virtually indestructible once established, spreading from root suckers.
sources: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Native Vines

common_name: Ox Eye Sunflower (False Sunflower, Smooth Oxeye)

planting.note: Direct sow in spring (or fall for best germination) in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; false sunflower self-seeds freely and establishes permanent colonies in suitable sites — a reliable long-blooming perennial wildflower that is one of the most important midsummer native pollinator plants.

directions: Direct sow in fall or early spring in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; thin to 18–24 inches. Heliopsis helianthoides (false sunflower or ox eye) is a native prairie and woodland-edge perennial producing masses of 2-to-3-inch, bright-yellow daisy flowers with yellow or yellow-brown cone centers from late June through September — a blooming duration of 8–10 weeks that makes it one of the most valuable summer-blooming natives for pollinators, including bumble bees, native sweat bees, and monarch butterflies. Unlike true sunflowers (Helianthus), false sunflower is reliably perennial, returning year after year and gradually expanding from a central clump while self-seeding moderately. The cultivar 'Loraine Sunshine' (white-marbled leaves with green veins) is grown as much for foliage as flowers; 'Summer Nights' has deep-orange flowers; 'Prairie Sunset' has coppery-orange blooms on bronze-tinged foliage. It tolerates clay soil, drought, and brief flooding better than many comparable native perennials.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring or fall in very well-drained, dry to moderately moist, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; Gaillardia aestivalis is native to open, sandy or rocky prairies and dry pinelands across the Gulf Coastal Plain and is distinctly more heat- and drought-tolerant than garden blanketflower (G. pulchella or G. x grandiflora).

directions: Direct sow in well-drained to dry, lean, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; never plant in rich, moist soil, which causes rapid decline. Gaillardia aestivalis (prairie or lance-leaf blanketflower) is a native perennial blanketflower of the Gulf Coastal Plain — found in sandy pinelands, open prairies, and dry roadsides from North Carolina to Texas — that differs from the commonly grown annual/hybrid blanketflower (G. pulchella, G. x grandiflora) in being a true long-lived perennial in zones 6–10 and more specifically adapted to the hot, dry, sandy soils of the Gulf South. The flowers are typical blanketflower style — daisy-form with red ray petals tipped yellow, surrounding an orange-red disk center — but are borne more sparsely on taller, more open plants than the hybrid types. It blooms from late spring through fall and is an important nectar source for native bees and several butterfly species. It is distinctly more heat-tolerant and longer-lived in zone 8–10 conditions than most blanketflowers, which often decline after their first year in Gulf Coast heat.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Native Wildflowers

common_name: Ivy Leaf Morning Glory (Ivy Morning Glory)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost danger in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; nick or soak seeds for 12–24 hours to improve germination; ivy leaf morning glory self-seeds prolifically and can become invasive in warm climates — pull unwanted seedlings when small.

directions: Nick or file seeds and sow directly after last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; provide a trellis or fence for the twining stems. Ipomoea hederacea (ivy leaf morning glory) is a native North American morning glory producing ivy-shaped, three-lobed leaves that immediately distinguish it from common morning glories with heart-shaped foliage; the 1.5-to-2-inch flowers are sky-blue to pale blue to violet with a white throat, appearing profusely from summer through frost. The ivy-shaped foliage is the key ornamental distinction — it creates a finer texture and more elegant twining pattern on fences and trellises than the typical morning glory. This species is native to the eastern US and tropics and naturalizes readily in disturbed soils, roadsides, and old fields; it self-seeds abundantly and can spread aggressively in the landscape. It is valuable as a wildlife plant — the flowers provide nectar for bumblebees and the seeds feed native sparrows and mourning doves through winter. Zone 9 and warmer: treat as a warm-season annual that self-seeds reliably.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Native Morning Glories
